explanation: 'Tends to / often ends up': verb stem or noun + がち marks a recurring, usually undesirable tendency — 休みがち (often absent), 病気がち (sickly). It's about what happens repeatedly over time, while 気味 is a slight state right now (風邪気味 'a touch of a cold' vs 風邪をひきがち 'catches colds a lot'), and っぽい is a casual '-ish' about quality or appearance. It behaves like a な-adjective (がちな N・がちだ).
formation: V-ます stem・N + がち（だ・な・の）

examples.jp: <ruby>冬<rt>ふゆ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>風邪<rt>かぜ</rt></ruby>をひきがちなので、<ruby>気<rt>き</rt></ruby>をつけている。
examples.en: I tend to catch colds in winter, so I'm careful.

examples.jp: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>子供<rt>こども</rt></ruby>のころ、<ruby>病気<rt>びょうき</rt></ruby>がちだった。
examples.en: He was often sick as a child.

examples.jp: <ruby>最近<rt>さいきん</rt></ruby>、<ruby>仕事<rt>しごと</rt></ruby>が<ruby>忙<rt>いそが</rt></ruby>しくて、<ruby>朝<rt>あさ</rt></ruby>ごはんを<ruby>抜<rt>ぬ</rt></ruby>きがちだ。
examples.en: Lately work has been busy, and I tend to skip breakfast.

examples.jp: <ruby>一人暮<rt>ひとりぐ</rt></ruby>らしだと、<ruby>野菜<rt>やさい</rt></ruby>が<ruby>不足<rt>ふそく</rt></ruby>しがちになる。
examples.en: When you live alone, you tend to run short on vegetables.
